name: Almy, Warren Wright
motto: "Do as I say, not as I do." - John Calvin.
clubs: Deputation Team Y. M. C. A. So. S.
President Y. M. C. A., J.
Y. M. C. A. Cabinet F. So. J. S.
description: I am greatly indebted to the editor for having allowed me to prepare my own write-up in this famous volume, for I think that I alone am qualified to do justice to myself. Until I become fully occupied with my arduous labors in connection with my pastorate, I admit that I was the most prominent man in the class, and would no doubt have continued so had I been able to devote my executive ability to such inconsiderable affairs. However, I have always endeavored to lend empressment to all games and social functions by attending whenever the press of business relaxed sufficiently to permit it. I will probably attend a theological seminary after I get my degree, and may possibly return to Baltimore to preach. If so, I will be delighted to grant an audience to those members of the class whose characters are of sufficiently high moral tone to warrant such liberty.

name: Andrae, Karl Henry
motto: "The firste vertue, sone, if thou wilt lerne, Is to restreyne and kepen thy tonge." - Chaucer.
description: Here is one of those persons who knows everything about everybody. He knows the histories of your own relatives better than you do yourself. He is a good mixer and will talk to you about anything. The only thing is, you never know what he is talking about, for his delivery is very intricate and erratic. It takes an acrobatic mind to follow him because an unrelated comment upon some incident of the far past may be injected at any time. In fact, we firmly believe that only constant association makes him understandable. This youth is a source of mystery to the teachers, who cannot decide whether he is a genius, a mischievous kid, or a man with an ego as large as himself. It is true that he is self-confident, but we admit that he has some cause to be so, for he is an industrious worker with an active brain. He has sound ideas and a good business sense. Above all, Karl has the kindest of hearts and is most generous with his help to all who ask it.

name: Frank Appelstein
motto:
"An honest man, close-buttoned to the chin,
Broadcloth without, and an honest heart within."—*Cowper*.
clubs: Class Football, Track and Lacrosse, F. So.
description: Hark Ye! Above notice Apps of the chubby face and curly hair. He looks handsome, but he had a good photographer who can do wonders. Frankie's features may not be all there, but he has a good disposition and a variety of good accomplishments. As a football player we hate to rate him; as a chemist we will be mum about him; but as a politician—oh, boy!—he sure does shine. Up to election and months thereafter the class did no work. Apps claimed all their time, and sad to say after election the class had to pitch in and buy his meals for the rest of the year. But Apps still claims to be a wonderful politician and his release from the confinement of his studies bodes ill for the peace of mind of Kelly, Mahon and the other estimable gentlemen who are deciding the fate of the nation.

name: Eugene Stanley Ault
motto:
"Music, I yield to thee
As swimmer to the sea,
I give my spirit to the flood of song."—*Henry Van Dyke*.
description:
We have nothing but good to say about Stan. In the first place, he does his own work, which is more than a lot can say. He is usually up to date, and, furthermore, he manages to keep his laboratory partners pretty well in hand. His kindness of heart is shown by the willingness with which he loaned us his best long overcoat, after that stormy afternoon in the E. E. Lab. when Defie ripped the seat off our official trousers. In the years we have known him, we have never seen him get fussed up, or, as "Chawlie" used to say, lose his equanimity. He plays the violin, he can sing bass, but he loves most of all to dance and to get up dances. We know that he is a good business man from the efficient way in which he manages the latter.
May his toes never become tangled with his technical transmutations!
Thirty-one

name: Eli Baker
motto: "Turn him to any cause of policy, The Gordian knot of it he will unloose." — Henry V.
clubs: Class Lacrosse and Football, F., So.
description: It is no secret that Eley is going to devote his career to invention. The first device that he will patent will be his wrenchless locknut, the inspiration for which he conceived while preparing to be a lieutenant in the artillery at Fort Monroe. The one thing that worries him is the problem of obtaining sufficient salesmen to effectually market his products. His acquaintances would not be surprised, however, if Bake should forsake the world of science for that of politics. A masterly stroke was observed in last fall's campaign, whereby he and Torsch formed a ring, elected each other to high office, and divided the proceeds. As one of our most vigilant upholders of the truth, he's always ready to argue about any phase of our activities that may need reforming, and as for his labors on the Student Council for the abolition of the Senior June examinations, they will go down in history with those of Hercules and William Jennings Bryan.

name: John Ries Bartels
motto: "A thing of beauty is a joy forever." — Keats.
clubs: Varsity Track Team, So., J. S.
Member R. O. T. C., F., So.
Y. M. C. A. Cabinet, J. S.
description: Bang! One more lap to go. The crowd in the stands gives forth volumes of boisterous noise for our captain. An unexpected sprint at the opportune moment and Bartels breaks the tape. Then our illustrious captain grabs his robe from one of his many adoring attendants, throws it over his shoulders, and struts off the field in the dignified manner which is peculiarly his own. So it goes race after race throughout the season. He has hundreds of other accomplishments. He is an ardent Y. M. C. A. diplomat. In fact, the sight of John in the midst of his preacher gang discussing some trifling matter with the utmost seriousness while his chief rivals in deviltry, Bill Merriken and the Hon. George Cattanach, listen attentively, is a very common one on the campus. In this, as in everything else, John has always been a leader, and has had no inconsiderable share in making the short sojourn of 1920 at Hopkins a significant one.

name: Beall, Paul Uburto
motto: "The days of my freedom are over, and the life of a bachelor ends."—Kipling.
clubs: Corporal R. O. T. C., So.
description: Good old red-haired, freckle-faced Beall! If red hair stands for anything, it stands for unexcelled wit and humor. Reds was a good fellow until he got married in his Junior year—now he's a better fellow. He isn't much to look at as far as Greek beauty is concerned, but when you get him in a crowd "You'd be surprised." In spite of all his foolishness we've all got to admit there's something to Reds. He's the shining light of the class. (Ouch, that's horrible!) As far as a future is concerned, Reds is assured. (If he doesn't make good his wife will make him make good.) We are all looking forward to the time when he becomes Senator Beall, so we can point him out and say, "I graduated from Johns Hopkins with that man in 1920."

name: Berlin, Max
motto: "Since you, my friend, have tied my living tongue, With thanks more large than man e'er said or sung, So let the dumbness of this sketch be My eloquence, and still interpret me."—*Sidney Lanier.*
description: Max, or Dutch as he is more commonly known, is one of the best-hearted fellows in the world. He can't do enough for a friend. Owing to his name, he was joshed unmercifully during the past war, but like the genial chap he is, he stood it smilingly and showed his patriotism by joining Uncle Sam's ranks. Max was one of the old guard who was here when the Thompsonites reigned supreme in student life at Hopkins, and it is probably due to this severe yet wonderful experience that he has always been a real Hopkins man. He won his numerals in '18 in lacrosse and in various class elections has wielded no little political power. He expects to follow chemistry after he graduates and if history repeats itself, he will be a Lavoisier "sans doute"* *French.

name: Berry, Thomas Morris
motto: "The ladies call him sweet, The stairs, as he treads on them, kiss his feet."—*Love's Labor's Lost.*
description: When the, now famed and respected class of 1920 first ascended to the seats of learning, Nix was there in the front rank. Later we began to notice his galloping around the cross-country course at all hours, in all kinds of weather, and in all kinds of costumes. As time has gone on his speed has become more and more dazzling, and few of us will be surprised if he makes the Varsity Track Team this year. Nix really has great capacity for hard work. He has also a propensity for taking copious notes and the ability of impressing the professors with the idea that he is giving his whole and undivided attention to their lectures. In view of the qualities that he has exhibited, we believe that Nix is going to make this cold world a pretty warm place after all.

name: Bills, Charles Everett
clubs: Charter Member Social Science Club, J.
description: We have a young member named C. E. Bills, Who ventures a theory for curing all ills, From old Massachusetts he hails with the fame That he came from a city of Lawrence, by name. Now Charlie is destined a chemist to be For he's always contriving new compounds to see, But if you'll take a hint, why, I'm reasonably sure That girls have for Charlie a terrible lure. His interest is strong in questions of weight, As shown by his interest in coming at eight To listen to lectures by speakers galore On social conditions in old Baltimore. So here's to our Charlie in hope that he may Become quite a chemist and leader some day.

name: Bloomsburg, Harry Eugene
motto: "While there is life there is hope."
clubs: Y. M. C. A. Deputation, J.
Varsity Baseball Team, So. J. S.
description: When our poor benighted young freshmen looked on page 34 of the Y. M. C. A. Handbook, they acquired the following information - "Baseball Captain - Bloomsburg '20." And now 'tis he, even Harry - the wild young shortstop and captain of our diamond performers - who smiles at you from above. He has been out for the team since his sophomore year and for the last two has played on the varsity. Not only is he a wizard at picking 'em off his shoe laces, but he can also toss them around a bit. This young fellow has a wonderful future ahead of him as a chemical engineer, if he doesn't allow himself to be ruined by those wild Bolsheviks - Mersereau and Orne. Happy days to you Harry, and may all your troubles be little ones!

name: Albert Herbert Blum
motto: "A lock of hair, an egg, a kiss; There's not a bit of sense in this." - Goldsmith
clubs: R. O. T. C., F. S.
description: Al is one of the few men who has completed his course in a short time and not at the cost of sacrificing everything to books. I have often wondered why it is given to some men to be sufficiently able in study, proficient in athletics, and at the same time possessed of masculine magnetism, that potent something which is love's sine qua non and which leads love-lorn ladies to lay their hearts at one's feet. Al's advice and opinion on various phases of Barnett's hobby has been eagerly sought by many of the twinkling stars in that department. On the lacrosse field he's a bull, or whatever the vulgar expression is which conveys the meaning of superhuman skill. As to his third outstanding characteristic, we will not speak. Look at him! Suffice it to say that this favorite of the gods is inevitably bound for a nook in the Hall of Fame, to the glory of himself and of us all.

name: Daniel Baker Bratt
motto: "Here stand I tight and trim, Quick of eye, though little of limb; He who denieth the word I have spoken, Betwixt him and me shall lance be broken."
description: It is strange how a good cigar will take the ambition out of a man. Before he smoked, Dan used to show ambition, but now give him a soft chair and some Planter's Delight and he is satisfied. Smoking is not the sole cause of the ruination of our fair-haired friend. Down in Oxford, on the good old Eastern Shore, there's a -----. Well, never mind. Don't think that he has lost all his pep though. He has an enviable rep as a lightweight rough-houser and often takes on the heavies, usually to their misfortune. It has even been said that he holds the world's record for throwing shoes (not horseshoes, you hick) and making them land just where he wants. How about it, Nick? You ought to know.

name: Bresee, Fred
motto: "For he's a jolly good fellow."
clubs: Captain R. O. T. C., S.
Member R. O. T. C., So. J. S.
description: This original young man from Kansas is an intimate friend of everybody, and one whose presence always advances good humor a point or two. Although primarily a student, he excels in other activities besides French I. In the spring his fancy turns to "going out for lacrosse next year," but in military affairs he's a bear, all dressed up in his new uniform with the buckskin shock-absorbers and the nickel-plated shoulder pads. It has been alleged that Breezy intends to become a Bone Carpenter when he grows up. If this be true, we are sure that that profession will be highly benefited by his presence in its bosom, and that Pittsburg can still be assured of a place on the map. Whether he goes into medicine or continues to devote all his time to committee work, he sure has 20's best wishes for success.

name: Brumbaugh, John Daniel
motto: "Describe him who can / An Abridgement of all that is pleasant in man."
description: John D. is a native of Ohio, the Buckeye State, and has been with us only a year, coming here from the University of Wooster. He is a man of no mean ability and his rather serious, industrious nature has gained for him a scholastic record that is to be envied. The Chemistry and Electrical Engineering laboratories are his daily habitats, and as an evening's pastime he solves Physics problems. He claims immunity from the wiles of the fair sex, and gets most of his social diversion among his masculine acquaintances. However, as Al Jolson has been heard to remark, he "can't win all the time." Brumbaugh is planning to go into scientific manufacturing as a life-work, and is thinking seriously of Massachusetts Tech. Whatever he does, we feel sure of his success, for whenever he attempts a thing, he does it with a concentration and thoroughness that is bound to produce results.

name: Burger, Thomas Terry
motto:
"Come and trip it as you go
On the light fantastic toe."
clubs: Member R. O. T. C., F. So. J.
Major R. O. T. C. S.
Vice-President Y. M. C. A., S.
Secretary-Treasurer Omicron Delta Kappa Society, S.
description: Teddy's smile is one of the pleasantest sights around Hopkins and is good to see on a rainy day. Like Old Sol it warms and cheers our hearts, but unlike him, he has never been known to set. But this is just the cheerful accompaniment to his many activities, for Teddy B. is a regular "boarder" at Hopkins and has sat on more Boards than there are in the boardwalk at Atlantic City. Manager of Lacrosse, blushing Dolly Clandon of the play, sporting editor, wearer of the O. D. K., and one time President of our illustrious class are but a few of the many honors he has held. As presiding officer at class-meetings he was a wonderful mob-leader, where his insistent gavel always fell unheeded. "Miles of Smiles" has brought Ted never-failing popularity; so "smile the while we bid you fond adieu."

name: Calkins, Jr., Frederic Russell
motto: "Give it to Eric!" — Ye Olde Play.
clubs: Varsity Football Team, So. J. S.
description: The sensation of the football year in Maryland was the sudden and dramatic appearance of Calkins as the "best back in the State." Latent powers under a New England reserve proved such a successful combination that the incessant demand of the stands was "Give it to Calkins." Eric is very quiet and reserved, but, at least in Stony's class, silence spoke louder than words, for the above-mentioned lately-departed was frequently heard to expatiate in warmest terms upon the high character of that fellow Calkins. Paradoxical as it may seem, off the football field Eric is somewhat slow in getting into action mentally, but he will no doubt make his way in the world by remedying the mistakes of those who work too fast.

name: Mitchell B. Carroll
motto:
"Here with a Loaf of Bread beneath the Bough,
A Flask of Wine, a Book of Verse
And Wilderness is Paradise enow."—Omar Khayyam.
clubs: Corresponding Secretary Y. M. C. A., J.
First Lieutenant, Battalion Adjutant, R. O. T. C., J.
description: Let not the above verse give the impression that Mitch is lazy or that he has ever shirked his duty in any way. His achievements in Hopkins before he was called to the war bear eloquent witness to his masterly editorial ability and his Demosthenaic skill. Mitch left college in his soph year to enter the air service. It is generally understood that he was returning from an afternoon call when he fell into a day dream, and his ship into a tail spin. Being only a few hundred feet up, the tail spin terminated shortly, but Mitch dreamed in the Officers' Hospital for many more days. Since his return he has divided his time between serious study and writing beautiful verse, excerpts from which we have obtained the permission of his publisher to print herein. Some day, who knows, we may all be worshipping at the shrine of this exponent of the lawless lyric.

name: Harry Joseph Casey
motto: "No person in the crowd could doubt 'Twas Casey at the bat."—Favorite Rhyme.
description: Our friend Harry entered our illustrious class in his junior year with an ensign's uniform, an ever-present smile, and a consummate loyalty to the General Electric Company. He has at least two talents which mark him as a future president of the General Electric Company. First, he has a remarkable adaptability for salesmanship and the ability to boost that Company even in the disgusted presence of those professors who are loyal to a rival concern. Besides this one indication of the future, we see something else which is as yet rather hazy in the crystal ball. However, we can now begin to faintly discern the vague outlines of the cottage of love with no kitchen and a little garden with roses growing everywhere. After saying this the seer can see no more, either because of blindness or a sense of delicacy.

name: Cattanach, George Scholl
motto: "Hear now a song A song of broken interludes Of a singer nothing worth."—Kipling.
clubs: R. O. T. C. F. So. J. S.
1st Lieutenant, Battalion Adjutant, R. O. T. C., S.
Chief Usher Commemoration Day, So. J. S.
College Representative Alumni Magazine, So. J. S.
description: "Say fellows!" That is the way George prefaces all his remarks and he usually has something to say about everything, whether it is worth saying or not. His loquaciousness is stilled only in "Slony's" philosophy class, and only then because he could not hope to compete with such lunatic ranters as Foard (the "a" has been introduced so as not to be confused with the microbes of that name) and "Louie" Macht. Maybe you didn't know it, but George has been offered a contract as a tenor with the Cosmopolitan Opera Company. This happened after he sang that well-known lullaby entitled, "How Clara Cured Clarence's Colic with Mrs. Winslow Soothing Syrup." I heard G. S. sing it myself at the Tome concert, and I guarantee it will cure if it doesn't kill. Besides George plays first fiddle in the orchestra and he is some sawer. He plays all that highbrow stuff like Tsch—well anyway it ends like Bolsheviki. With all these talents he's bound to succeed in the Medical School.

name: Coleman, Aylett Breckenridge Jr.
motto: "A man may hold all sorts of posts If he'll only hold his tongue."—Kipling.
clubs: Secretary-Treasurer Omicron Delta Kappa Society, J.
Captain R. O. T. C., S.
Committee on Prep. School Visiting, J. S.
Member of Cast "The Admirable Crichton," S.
description: Abbie and five Presidents of our beloved country have hailed from Virginia, but Abbie, unlike the natives of the Old Dominion, is blest with the modesty of the truly great. Although at first unaware of the genius within its midst, as the years rolled by 1920 has come to admire the pen and shun the cube of Coleman. With his pen he has written himself into the hearts of his class-mates, and with his cube he has thrown himself into their pockets. That Abbie is an all-around man is shown by his military honors, his ability in shaking the shimmie, and his knowledge of the female character displayed in his interpretation of Agatha in the recent show. We are sure he will never antagonize the judges of the courts of Roanoke, but having soothed them first with his modest likeable manner he will proceed to conquer their minds with his irresistible logic.

name: Collins, John Harding
motto: "With a heart free from sickness and sorrow. And while peace and plenty I find at my board." —John Collins, of Birmingham Daily Chronicle, died 1808.
description: We all used to imagine that Johnny was our original hard-luck man. In his sophomore term the company football series left him for a time with only half his wits, and a year later he came off the field with only one foot. His jinx is now broken, however, because this year his noble kicking for the champion senior M.E.-E.E. aggregation helped them roll up their magnificent score against the C.E.-Chems. Johnny is always ready to tell us the latest news from the Eastern Sho', as is set forth in the Chestertown "Blaetter." When this runs out he can usually run us a little inside dope on how it "was done in Chile," which he visited last summer while an officer on one of the Peruvian Barks of the merchant marine.—(Uh-huh, Chief Engine Wiper's Assistant's Helper.) Some day Johnny will return triumphantly to Chestertown. On that occasion the leading citizens will hold a parade, and the band at the head will play, "Here comes the conquering hero!"

name: Cox, Richard Threlkeld
clubs: 1st Sergeant, R. O. T. C., So.
Instructor, R. O. T. C., J., S.
description: O poet—shall no one sing of thee, Who sings so well of other men? Scion of Southern chivalry, Tho' poor the wit and weak the pen, Shall no one count thy vertebrae Or talents or variety? He writes, and Homer looks askance To see a rival bard arise. He draws—and draws an envious glance From Raphael's half-averted eyes. While Darwin knows he'll have no chance Against the Cox's swift advance. O Kenyon Cox, thy sun is set, Great critic, you must say farewell, Wise man whom no one can forget, You're dead, so creep into your shell. Artists, his loss you may regret, But dry your eyes and do not fret, For Richard Cox is with us yet.

name: Philip Charles Craft
motto:
"A cigarette, a cigarette
My kingdom for a cigarette."
description: Phil Craft is an aristocrat, a man of parts, a student of penetration, and that rare composite animal, the social lion, tea hound, and lounge lizard. At dances he is rather the feline didymus. He is very successful with women and is seldom seen save in the company of a beautiful girl. His capacity for work and for—but pardon, we were about to employ an archaic word—is hyper-average. He is the one man who never took his siestas with Pop Vincent, or his military training with the corps, or his tennis opponents seriously. Tennis is his game as are certain other games, with which he is no less lucky than in love. He has been known to state two anomalous desires—to be a college professor and to be a rich man. But whatever he attains to—a wealth of knowledge or a knowledge of wealth, Phil always will be what he is: patrician, Nietzschean, gentleman.

name: William Kennedy Cromwell, Jr.
motto:
"A faithful horse!
Wild oats he never sowed
Yet masticated tame ones with much zest
Cheerful he bore each light allotted load
As cheerful took rest." —Ye Olde Play.
clubs: Delegate to Inter-Fraternity Board, F. S.
description: A typical country gentleman, old Cromwell, next to his ease, loves a dog, a gun, and a sight at quail or railbird best of all. His ponderous, earth-shaking snore can be heard in all his classes. This strenuous work over, he goes at once to his home at Lake Roland to watch his bees storing honey, to apply mange cure to his dogs, or, if it is autumn, to finish his doze before an open fire. The old boy was somewhat cut up by the prohibition amendment, for his dream of happiness has been to sip a mint julep and watch his shadow go round the house. He named his cow United States because it was dry. Some day Cromwell wants to marry the right girl and start farming. Of course, he doesn't intend to do any farm work. He rather fancies the ideal pastoral existence of the Forest of Arden. We wish him happiness, teeming acres, shady trees, babbling brooks, humming bees, and barrels of cider.

name: Harold Wilson Dail
motto: "It is extremely difficult to hit the precise point where true wit ends and buffoonery begins."—Hugh Blair.
clubs: R. O. T. C., F. So. J. S.
1st Lieutenant, R. O. T. C. S.
Assistant Manager Lacrosse Team, F. So.
description: To say that Harold is a good boy—a successful student, a congenial acquaintance, a worthwhile friend, an irresistible lover, is merely to make a declaration; but to say that he came to Baltimore from the Eastern Shore at an early age (youthful intelligence), that those whom he may count as his friends are countless, that even the maelstrom of Psychology I left his average untroubled—that his acquaintances all call him Bing, which means in the Lithuanian “fiery, courageous,” that his favorite expression concerns the alleviation of coryza for one’s costiveness, that not content with an education he goes seeking a profession as well at Harvard Law School—is to prove it. If he tackles everything with the intriguing cheeriness that has made him famous at Hopkins, the sky alone’s the limit, and we may live to see the glorious day when as Chief Justice of the Supreme Court, Bing shall declare the Eighteenth Amendment unconstitutional.

name: Edgar Sterling Daugherty
motto: "Though gentle, yet not dull. Strong without rage, without o'erflowing full."—Dryden.
description: What would the M. E.'s have done this year without their "Team of Hosses?" Our photograph does scant justice to his general personality. Whenever there is a battle in prospect, Ed. holds the front-line trenches; whenever Baker has a reform to launch, our Team throws his stabilizing influence into the balance. Long live Crisfield, the social center of the region that includes Denton and Chestertown! Salute our Varsity goal-keeper; he fills the bill and the goal. It was Daugherty that made the winning touchdown in the famous M. E.—E. E.—C. E.—Chem. football game, from Bake's intercepted forward pass—long will he be honored for it! It was he who helped rescue our banner after the State football game, and he who gave himself the night before in pursuit of the white billy-goat. Yea, he studies in the same noble manner. Gentlemen, be seated. man, old Cromwell dog, a gun, and a best of all. His per- re can be heard in mous work over, he at Lake Roland to ney, to apply many is autumn, to finish re. The old boy was prohibition amend- ppiness has been to atch his shadow go med his cow United Some day Crom- right girl and start doesn't intend to do ner fancies the ideal e Forest of Arden teeming acres, shady humming bees, and Forty-three

name: Hamilton Chace Davis
motto: "Awake, arise, or be forever fallen."—Milton.
clubs: R. O. T. C. F. So.
Instrumental Clubs, F. So. J. S.
description: Squeak! Squeak! regularly at twenty-five minutes of nine Davis's little kiddy car motorcycle turns in at the Homewood entrance, ignores Goodnow's traffic regulations, and lands Ham safe if not sane in Thatch's classroom. This one lapse from the road of virtue, to say nothing of the way that cycle laps up the road, is the only departure he allows himself. In all other things he is a model of good deportment. Major Guild, a veritable ferret for recognizing merit, early seized upon this youth and marked him for his own. His has been the gigantic task of making supple the bodies of the would-be soldiers, for in calisthenic instruction in the R. O. T. C. he has been supreme. Having ignored Polly Tics and devoted himself strictly to Polly Con, Davis is one of the most inoffensive and popular men around Hopkins. It is the sincere wish of his class that his life may be one long cycle of enjoyment.

name: Francis Marion Defandorf
motto: "Thyrsis, the music of that murmuring spring Is not so mournful as the strains you sing."—Pope.
clubs: Lacrosse Squad, F., So., J., S.
Class Track and Football, F., So.
description: Defie, although extremely short in stature, is as wide in good nature as his ever-present million-dollar smile, the smile that should cause any girl to leave her happy home. Even though we turn his desk over, hang his bed out of the window without first removing him (to the great delight of passing Goucherites), or even copy his railways problems, which he doesn't hand in, he has never been known to lose his temper. Another admirable quality which everyone appreciates is his deep sense of modesty. Of all the years that he has been bass canary for the musical clubs he has never been known to burst spontaneously into song when in the presence of others. This characteristic, as you all know, is not possessed by all. In conclusion, we may state that Francis Marion (it is not wise to call him thusly to his face), although somewhat hampered by his studies, has found time to play lacrosse, and many an opponent has gone down before his terrific fifty-eight inch onslaught.

name: Donohoe, Edward
motto: "Be slow in choosing a friend, slower in changing."—Benjamin Franklin.
description: Ed or Don as he is generally called, is one whose aspirations are for a degree in medicine. Just like so many of the Pennsylvania Dutch, Don has a tendency to be a bit lazy, but nevertheless he has gone through the Pre-medical course in three years without much exertion. He is somewhat shy—except with the girls about town—and rather conservative. Whether his pipe makes him so or not is a question, for he never seems to separate himself from it. It comes to the University in the mornings at 8:30, and leaves with him after lab at 4. Don's great and inseparable friend is Eastland. They can generally be found in the biology lab cutting cats or just talking. Don's quiet ways have won him many friends and we wish him all success in the medical world.

name: Driver, Jr., Warren Maynard
motto: "Whose public works will best attest his fame Whilst private worth adds value to his name."—Lyall.
clubs: Member S. A. I. A. A.
1st Lieutenant R. O. T. C. J.
description: When interviewed by our representative, Warren M. Driver, Jr., said in part... We are glad he said it only in part, for if he were to write his autobiography we would have to get out another edition of THE HULLABALOO. Only a veritable Boswell, who would follow him everywhere and jot down his most minute thoughts, could do justice to his importance. By the side of Driver, Goodnow and Latané fade into insignificance, and it is well-known that, next to Leber and Roulston, he is the most overworked man at Hopkins. Silver-tongued and insistent in debating, dictatorial in HULLABALOO management, verbose in reporting, he has also earned the dignified title of "Old Lady Driver" of the track team. Poor, blind, misguided Wanlass insisted on adding to Driver's fame the reputation of a banking expert "versed in the various phases and functions of banking activity."

name: Winfield Henry Emlet
motto: "The Glee Club of Johns Hopkins University, of which Winfield H. Emlet, son of Mr. and Mrs. J. C. Emlet, of Carlisle Street, is a member, filled an engagement at Port Deposit, Md., Saturday evening."—Society Note.
clubs: Corporal in the S. A. T. C.
description: This specimen of the Pennsylvania Dutchman hails from Hanover, Pa., where they make the celebrated shoes which he wears. "Flop" gets away with a supposed knowledge of Chemistry and many other subjects at the University. He gloried in his position when in charge of a squad room as a Corporal in the S. A. T. C. This year he has delighted many audiences by his chesty appearance when singing second base on the Glee Club. He was an active member of the infamous combination known as "Pop, Flop and Jazz, Inc." during its turbulent existence. His broad smile and swinging gait are familiar not only to our campus, but also down on Maryland Avenue, where he is reputed to be popular with several fair Goucherites. And so he is wherever he goes.

name: Benjamin Engleman
motto: "His hair is of a good color."—Shakespeare.
description: Red, as he is best known, appears to be a quiet, unassuming young fellow, but in reality he is some sport with the ladies. (At least he thinks so.) He is a member of that terrible bunch of Chem. Engineers, Engleman, Berlin, and Folkoff, who have offices at the Maryland and on Lexington Street. And by the way, in speaking of sports, Red at one time thought that he could play the ponies and get away with it, but it appears that he had the wrong dope. As for chemistry, he admits that he is some chemist and we feel confident that some day he will become chief chemist with the Dupont people, or even do consulting work for that young and growing corporation, Orne, Bloomsburg & Co.

name: Evans, George Heberton Jr.
motto: "Call on a business man at business times only, transact your business; go about your business, in order to give him time to finish his business."—Grammar School Spelling Book.
clubs: Sergeant R. O. T. C. So.
President S. A. T. C. Association, J.
Acting Graduate Manager of Athletics, J.
description: So congealing is the dignity of Heb that we can't imagine anyone cracking a joke at his expense even for THE HULLABALOO, unless it would be the world-famous joke of posing as a woman-hater. His dignity is only equalled by his efficiency, for he is one of the most capable men in the class—capable even of being a good first sergeant in the S. A. T. C. In that organization, the green shavetails from Plattsburg were glad to turn to Heb for pointers in military drill. For several years Evans handled the position of Graduate Manager of Athletics and had an office of his own, which added to his dignity. He wears the H. M. for managing the football team this year in good style. He says his ambition is to go off to distant parts in search of adventure. We hope he won't encounter the terrible dragon of the Faerie Queene, for which Ma Greene looks under her bed every night, but we cannot help wishing that he will fall desperately in love with some fairy queen, who will pull him down from his pinnacle of reserve.

name: Foard, Francis Millard
motto: "A harmless necessary evil ranting right along."—From "Mutilations" of Shakespeare.
clubs: Safety Committee for Gilman Hall, S.
Assistant Chief Usher Commemoration Day, S.
Φ B K, O Δ K
description: The wisdom of a Solomon, Adonis-like beauty, Demosthenaic loquacity, and Falstaffian wit have never been more aptly blended than in this verbose, thesaurus-like edition of man prosodically termed Foard. Millard is the Don Juan of all the girls at B. C. C., who do nothing but adore him while he is trying to drill them in typewriting. The meteoric and pyrotechnical display of Foard's command of the English language is sufficient to outshine all the greater powers that be. He simply kills them cold with words, words—God how he raves! The arguments that Foard has up his sleeve would outwit Pallodino or Sir Oliver himself. He looks perfectly normal most of the time, except when he is a swearing, work-maddened Tantalus, engrossed in preparing the HULLABALOO. Beware then! For he has the wrath of Achilles. But come, a little praise is due. Indeed it is only through the untiring efforts of Foard that THE HULLABALOO is a success. He is a leader in college activities and spares no means to make our University the best in the country.

name: Folkoff, Joseph Paul
motto: "Thy wit is as quick as the greyhound's mouth—it catches."—Much Ado About Nothing.
description: From his prominent forehead you would mark him out as our next Edison, but those who know him best consider this only a slight deformity. He received his preparatory training in New York and came to Hopkins imbued with such Bolshevik ideas as coeducation and no examinations. On account of our famous climate, however, he soon sobered, and became a regular Hopkins rooter. Joe was once headed for the mechanical engineering profession, and not until his third year did he realize that his latent talents for mixing could best be developed in the chemistry department. His M. E. friends may forgive his turning Benedict Arnold, but success in his new field alone can clear his conscience. And success—well, why should he lose his wavy crop worrying about it? Didn't he get 9's and 10's without studying?

name: Gardner, William Maslin
motto: "Life is a gamble."
clubs: Lacrosse Squad, F. So. J. S.
Cotillion Board, F. So. J. S.
description: One of the most frequently-put questions at Hopkins during the past four years has been "How does Gardner get away with it?" In his ability to maintain an air of super-intelligence upon practically no preparation he has had only one rival—I mean the incomparable Bartow. This is all by way of conclusive proof that Dame Fortune, in common with sundry other ladies, has been, is, and apparently always will be, on the side of Billy. A man of his prodigious endowment of good looks, likeability, and dancing skill is surely selected by the Fates to tread the primrose-bordered path through life. He has always held himself somewhat aloof and hence has not had due share in the limelight of class honors, but he takes with him into the arena of life the friendship of many and the enmity of none.

name: Glick, Samuel
motto: "View the whole scene, with critic judgment scan, And then deny him merit if you can. Where he falls short, 'tis Nature's fault alone, Where he succeeds, the merit’s all his own."—Churchill.
description: Yes, you quack psychologists, cast your searching eyes for nineteen years at the face above and dare tell us that physical size has anything to do with intellectual progress, with the ability to gain popularity, etc. As he himself would say, "Legs don’t make the man." And right he is, for we know many a physically hyperbolized person who succumbs to faint-heartedness at the mere mention of blood and women. Well, suffice it to say, the sight of disarticulated animals, no matter how big they look (under the microscope), and the love sobs of any number of harmless women cannot move our little hero. For with his musical talent, his abundance of roaring wit and humor, not to speak of his scholastic ability, this diminutive medicine-man-to-be is a real winner. "Good goods comes in small packages," and in Sam "Shipley" Glick we can boast of sending out some gifty package to "whoever is concerned."

name: Griesacker, Joseph Benedict
motto: "I bear the burden and the heat Of the long day and wish 'twere done."—Matthew Arnold.
description: What other people accomplish by obtrusive handshaking and vociferous professions of friendship, Joe accomplishes by quietly and decently doing the right thing at the right time. Not that he is by any means a Chesterfield, although to be sure he has assimilated enough of those vile tubes of that denomination to make a W. C. T. U. member start five or six campaigns. Joe was particularly adept in history, always contriving by an air of bland understanding to completely dupe Doc Vincent, a feat which (as everyone who ever attended his classes knows) is very, very difficult. Joe's favorite occupation has been to drape himself picturesquely in one of the deep, soft chairs in the Barn and devote himself to an assiduous cultivation of the shy god of sleep. He has cultivated friendships here that will be very lasting and which indicate qualities in him that will do much to help him carry on.

name: Griffiss, Bartow
motto: "Undoubtedly there is a right way of reading, so it be sternly subordinated. Books are for the scholar's idle time."—Emerson.
clubs: R. O. T. C., F. So. J.
Sergeant R. O. T. C., So. J.
description: Bartow has our universal admiration. When he receives his diploma in June he completes the greatest feat in the history of Johns Hopkins. This eminent political economist, disciple of Griffin and Barnett, the apple of Wallass' eye, has never purchased a text-book in all these years, and it is rumored that he has never "cracked" one. We don't know what to attribute it to unless it is his matinee-idol appearance. He has become a veritable criterion of fashion with a stunning fancy vest and woolen socks that talk. His favorite occupation, if he may be said to have one, is gently polishing his mahogany shoes until he can contemplate his "economic well-being" in their shining tips.

name: Henck, Louie Wheeler
motto: "Maidens' hearts are always soft."—Bryant.
description: Now we have the city of Frederick represented. Great was the lamentation when this young ball-room floor-polisher left town to become a B. t. u. chaser in Mr. John Hopkins University down there in Baltimore. In our fair town, the home of hospitals, beautiful women, and recalcitrant city councils, his reputation was borne out. At every dance, Louie answered "here" to the roll call. He says when work interferes with dancing, cut out work. Mention girls to Louie and he'll rave about 'em, or about a "her." He is very diplomatic though; we don't know whether Baltimore girls or Frederick belles are his preference. Townsend says, "Why worry?" His object in life is to apply modern principles of engineering to Western Maryland soda fountains. We all wish him lots of luck in his work; he'll need it.

name: Herring, Francis William
motto: "A friend who's worth all hazards we can run."—Young.
clubs: Y. M. C. A., J., S.
description: Fish is another of our Fort Monroe Looies. We are proud of them, every one though no one else may be. Frank will enjoy that line, it has a true musical ring. In fact, he is a great lover of good music; that is why he doesn't care much about the Glee Club. One of his pet diversions is trying to make the Hydrogen Sulphide Tenor Trio go flat while rehearsing in his room. His expressive face is often seen around the Lyric, and frequently, it must be admitted, in the Parkway. Frank is a strong element of Professor Gregory's lacrosse squad, and likewise an indefatigable trench-digger, although Doc. Abercrombie gave up all hope for him in his freshman year. If he does not wear himself out from overwork or drown himself in one of his subterranean engineering masterpieces, we are sure that he will rise far towards the highest peaks of his chosen profession.

name: Hettleman, Milton Leonard
motto: "A sight to delight in."
description: If there is anyone in the university who has unity of purpose, it is Milton Hettleman. His sole aim at college is to learn to play chess. Daily Willy (so-called because he advertises spats for a local concern), peruses the Bulletin of Chess News at the Barn. The chess tournament results interest him immensely. Is Saiontz still leading? Hettleman must supersede him. Thereafter he concentrates all his energies upon the chess board. We suggest the establishment of a Department of Chess at the university for Willy's benefit. He is good at economics. He takes two "economic" courses. We believe that he takes these courses to formulate methods of controlling the congestion at Hazazers' Hall. Remember, dear Willy, chess, economics, jazz, and spats don't mix.

name: Hinrichs, Paul Christopher
motto: "The world is out of joint, O Cursed Spite That ever I was born to set it right."
clubs: H. A. A. Lacrosse, So. J.
Member of Cast "The Admirable Crichton," S.
description: We are told that during our span of life we will be called on to play many parts, but little did Paul think that when he came to Hopkins he would be called on to play the same part as his great namesake and become a preacher. Paul's own words "I'm not inclined in that direction." gives proof of his unpreparedness, yet when the call came, he did it and did it well, as all will testify, who saw him as Treherne, the sky pilot in "The Admirable Crichton." But do not think that this is the only part he can play, for Paul is essentially clever and versatile. He was one of the charter members of the Dramatic Club and has taken prominent parts in both of its productions. He was instrumental in ensuring the success of the newly-formed Literary Club. He has always been out for lacrosse and is one of Murphy's mainstays in the introduction of wrestling at Hopkins. In short, Paul is one of those valuable men who is really conscious of his duties to his college and his class and tackles everything with all his powers.

name: Hoffman, Robert Gilmor
motto: "A noble soul is like a ship at sea, That sleeps at anchor when the ocean's calm." —Beaumont and Fletcher.
clubs: Varsity "H", Football Team, F., So., J., S.
Lacrosse Team, F., So., J., S.
Swimming Team, F., So., J., S., Captain, So.
Secretary, Y. M. C. A., So., J.
Class Baseball, Lacrosse, Track, F., So.
description: Bobby needs no introduction to Hopkins followers, who will always remember him for his brilliant work on the gridiron and on the lacrosse field. He started off in the fall of his freshman year by landing a regular berth in the backfield of the football team, and in the following spring by becoming one of the mainstays of the lacrosse squad. At the outbreak of the war he entered the navy and soon rose to aviation circles, returning at the cessation of hostilities to resume his courses and to play again his usual steady and dependable part on the athletic field. Do not be misled, however, Bobby does not devote all of his time to athletics. He is an engineer, and, therefore, can be found any afternoon in the M. E. or C. E. lab., either chasing B. T. U.'s or designing a suspension bridge strong enough for him to flivver over to Paris. Bobby is, finally, a leader in student activities, and has distinguished himself as such in the various offices to which he has been elected by his fellow-students.

name: Hoffmeister, John Edward
motto: "Hail fellow well met." —Swift.
clubs: General Secretary Y. M. C. A., J.
Chairman Deputation Team, So., J., S.
Y. M. C. A. Cabinet, So., J., S.
description: This handsome chap is a member of that famous Sewing Circle (so-called because of the peculiar propensities of its members towards such "Apaches" as Merriken, etc.) of which Leber is the admirable pattern-maker and the "Y" is the mannikin with whom they promenade about the campus. Ed is evidently going as a missionary to far-off Yambuya on the Congo, if one may judge from the number of street vagabond conferences which he attends. His latest jaunt was out to Des Moines where, according to "Quacks from a Quack" (J. H. U. official society column), he made quite a hit with the feminine element. Girls, beware of this heart-breaker; he is only surpassed by his theosophical, Mt. Washington buddy. Whenever Ed is not on the warpath he may be found in Doc Gilpin's Chem. lab. It is his great ambition to be a chemist and save the reputation of the corner drug-store by concocting a spiriti fermenti incogniti. Ye disciples of Falstaff, behold your avatar. We wish you luck, old man.

name: Holland, Norman Norwood
motto: "And such pure dignity of love avowed, That in my eyes my full soul strove to speak." —Petrarch.
clubs: Lieutenant, R. O. T. C., S.
Engineering Football and Baseball, J., S.
description: Norman is one of those sweet souls, of whom we'll often think, when enjoying our pipe-dreams of the good old days. He has been always on hand to cheer and help us when beset by trials and tribulations, be they of the heart or of the classroom. He has never been known to fall by the wayside, and we are sure that a better student cannot be found. Though many changes have been wrought during his life at college, he has ever been the sedate country gentleman. A more critical inspection will reveal his crowning glory—a beautiful halo of corona—which has attracted to him many a kilowad, the anxious pursuit of whom has often carried him far into the tender hours of twilight. Aye, often would he scurry over to the post-office, to get the pink, sweet-scented letters of Cupid, and how his sweet countenance be lighted at the thought of his beloved. Thus, with the prophets predicting a great future for Norman and his electro-magnetic system of hair culture, we draw the curtain and leave Time to tell her tale.

name: Johnston, Lloyd Edward
motto: "From labor health, from health contentment springs." —Beattie.
description: 'Tis a beautiful spring evening. Supper is over, and there will be enough light for the next hour for the gentle sport of tennis. To the west still shines the sun; upon the clay court, appareled in spotless duck, shines our Johnny. From the way he whales the ball we know that he must be a shark at tennis. "Shave" (he was a second Looie from the Air Service when he came all the way from California to join us last spring) is one of the hard-working E. E.'s. We imagine that his success is due to the learned atmosphere he absorbed with Hancock, while bunking in Doc. Thomas' office last year. Although we haven't heard much about his dancing, we know that Johnston is quite a social lion around his boarding-house. We have still to find out why he bought a heart-shaped padlock for his desk. (We tried to open it once, and couldn't.) Fifty-five.

name: Kauffman, Lester S.
motto: "Smooth runs the water, where the brook is deep."—Shakespeare.
clubs: Captain Red Eagles Lacrosse Team, J.
description: This, ladies, is a portrait of a lacrosse-playing Eastern Shoreman. (See Encyclopedia Britannica for location of Eastern Shore.) To one who doesn't know him well, he is a very reticent and retiring young man. To his intimates, he surely is different. Why, we even remember when he played pinochle until 11 o'clock and smoked one of Shorty's Chesterfields. His one weakness is his absolute ruthlessness toward one who spells his name with one "f." But don't let this confidential earful of his dissipations prejudice you against him, for Dutch is a fine young man; even we who have borrowed his money think so. He doesn't shout very much, but—what is that old gag about still water? We predict that Dutch Kauffman will one day carve his initials into the pie crust of fortune.

name: Klass, Louis Howard
motto: "Oho! my Little Man, joy to you— And yours—and theirs—your lifetime through." —James Whitcomb Riley.
description: The door opened quietly, the curtains flapped and Klass was borne into our midst, in our senior year, on the wings of a Civil Engineering Breeze. Needless to say, we are all very glad that he was thus deposited with us, for, by his timely, witty sayings, he has enlivened many a passing hour and when we were sweating or freezing over some work for Pagon or Gregory. Klass has proven himself very useful in travelling to the Barn and chasing "hot dogs" and apples back to the waiting multitude. He is a great musician—playing, of course, the Klassics only—and he has the admirable quality of finishing his work first, especially if the latter should happen to be a problem in Structures.

name: Leber, Charles Tudor
motto: "I'll prove a busy actor in their play."—Shakespeare.
clubs: President Y. M. C. A., So. S.
description: Life is a great drama to Charlie, and he tackles it with all the ardor of the hero in the tensest moment of the play. When the organizers of the Hopkins Dramatic Club were desperately endeavoring to fill feminine roles with stalwart youths, amorous Charles, sure of the hero's part and disgusted at the unexciting prospect of loving such ladies, artfully suggested that real girls be included in the cast and finally said he would even prefer Goucher students to his bearded colleagues. Ah! Charlie, those love scenes, which made you Baltimore's matinee idol, were a bit too well done. Only experiences in the real thing could give that perfect poise and self-possession, and banish all suggestion of the amateur. Strange as it may seem, Leber, in his life-work, will forsake Melpomene and from the pulpit o' Sundays will use his gestures to point the way to Heaven. And won't the Ladies' Aid Society be loyal and enthusiastic?

name: Levin, Frank
motto: "He weren't no saint—them engineers is all pretty much alike."—Hay.
description: Frank joined our class in the junior year, returning to Hopkins after building aviation fields in California during the war. From his carpentering and hod-carrying work Frank realized that Civil Engineering was not his field, so when he returned he decided to be a mechanical. After a year of Jerry, he again reconsidered and when the senior year opened we found him an electrical. After he gets his degree he's going to forget all his engineering and run a straw-hat factory. Frank has his own ideas about everything and must be thoroughly convinced that they are absolutely wrong before he will change them. In E. E. I. laboratory his arguments with Torsch were so frequent and lengthy that we suspected that he was contemplating studying law on the side, but our suppositions have never been confirmed.

name: Levin, Max
motto: "Talk not of kings—I scorn the poor comparison, I am a sage, and can command the elements; At least men think I can, and on that thought I found unbounded empire."
description: Among the freshmen who were assembled in the Donovan Room on a fateful day in October, 1916, was a bright-eyed, wide-awake chap listening intently to the Dean. This freshman has proved to be, as expected, a most diligent student and a student of real worth to the college, for beside or in spite of the fact that he has always been a shark at recitations, he has managed to find time to take an interest in college activities. He has been one of the mainstays of the Zionist Society and is one of the best-informed men in the university on social questions. In addition to this, there are several features of which Max boasts besides his Roman nose, which have earned him the respect of all—namely, his friendliness, his willingness to do things for the benefit of his class, and his absolute fearlessness in standing up for what he deems best for all.

name: Levy, Raphael
motto: "For all men live and judge amiss Whose talents jump not just with his."
clubs: R. O. T. C., F. So.
description: The hero of this sketch is Monsieur Rafaelo Levy, the extinguished Romantic scholar of our fair University. Yes, Rafaelo believes in romance, as witness his affaire-du-coeur (via the mails), with a lady living 'way out in French Africa. (He has consistently refused to tell us whether the object of his devotion is white or otherwise, so draw your own conclusions.) Levy is a fairly hard-working fellow, with a liking for every subject he takes and an intense devotion to the Romance Languages, as proof of which we need but mention that he is this year taking courses in French, Italian and Spanish! What he intends to do with his lingual collection after he has amassed sufficient knowledge, or imitation of knowledge, to hoodwink the profs into giving him an A. B. is not yet known to the scribe. In fact, Ray himself does not know. But whether he decides to settle in French Africa or bring her here, he's sure to have our commiseration.

name: Lewin, John Henry
motto: "If he take you in hand, sir, with an argument, He'll bray you in a mortar."—Ben Jonson.
clubs: Sergeant, R. O. T. C., So.
description: John Henry, the man whom the Tome faculty would not part with till we had promised them Murray P. in exchange; the man who worsted Professor Smallwood (did we get the name right?) and the engineers in joint debate at our junior banquet of blessed memory; the devoted admirer of Magrene. Indeed, John's infatuation for old women is shown by his selecting two such parts in Hopkins plays, where his intimate knowledge of their ways—mayhap gleaned from his researches in English Literature—made him a hit. He is an excellent mimic, an orator, a poet, a prof. bluffer, and, moreover, editor of our Newsless-Letter. He intends to show Harvard how to really study law, and when he opens a speech in court by taking off his glasses in true Lady Brocklehurst fashion, the jury will surely capitulate.

name: Lippy, George Dewey
motto: "For thy sake, tobacco, I Would do anything but die."—Charles Lamb.
clubs: R. O. T. C., F. So. J.
Sergeant R. O. T. C., J.
description: Lippy has developed two strange passions while here: first, a wild, adoring love for things military, as exemplified, or rather as purported to be exemplified, in the R. O. T. C.; second, an equally wild, adoring friendship for Fred. Bresee. The two motives for endeavor thus given him have spurred him on to finish his job in three years rather than the customary four and have thus resulted in his being enrolled in these ranks of superhuman wisdom, celestial beauty, and mighty achievement. He has never burned any appreciable amount of midnight oil or suffered nervous breakdowns from over-study, but he has succeeded in all his courses in a very creditable fashion. In fact, our confidence in him is such that we will be perfectly willing to maintain against all comers that they spoiled a damn good lawyer when they made him take a pre-med course.

name: Lawrence Littman
motto: "Comb down his hair, look, look, It stands upright."
clubs: Member R. O. T. C., So.
Varsity Baseball Team, F. So. J. S.
description: Here's another of those baseball sharks—he can play with equal success anything from outfield to umpire, being especially proficient in the latter, owing to his fleetness of foot. Besides captaining last year's Baseball Team, Larry gave scope to his athletic prowess by playing on the famous independent basketball team, which enjoyed an entirely successful season, not being worsted in any of the fights in which they engaged. As a man of education, Larry, of course, doesn't count for much, for he is an engineer. But when it comes to using the slide rule and drawing queer figures, he is distinctly there. The potentates of the S. A. T. C., seeing that he knew too much, shipped him immediately to Fort Monroe where he is said to have spent most of his time at the Hotel Chamberlain. Keep it up, Larry, you'll be in the Big Leagues some day.

name: James Rosario Lomauro
motto: "O nose I am as proud of you As any mountain of its snows; I gaze on thee and feel that pride A Roman knows."
clubs: Member of Cast in "The Admirable Crichton"
R. O. T. C., So. J.
Y. M. C. A. Americanization Worker
description: Patience and perseverance will accomplish wonders. With this axiom in mind Lomauro has applied himself to pre-medical studies, heeling for the News-Letter, and conquering the co-eds and librarians around Hopkins. He comes to Homewood early in the morning and leaves late at night, devoting most of his time to talking with the fair sex. He always tells them that the interviews are for the News-Letter, which flatters them extremely; then this impartial beau accompanies each one home in turn, and on the way solicits their photos—to put in the News-Letter of course. The sly fox—we're sure he doesn't give all his conversations to the press, and as for the photos, wouldn't you like to have a squint at Jim's bureau? Poor girls, satisfied with the attentions of Beau Brummel, chant this answering refrain: There's a man named J. H. Lomauro Called today, he will drop in tomorrow I fear he's a crook For my photo he took Which I said he only might borrow.

name: Maccubbin, Walter Aubrey
motto:
"Aubrey Maccubbin, C. E.,
Is seven years junior to me.
Each bridge that he makes either buckles or breaks;
His work is as rough as he."—Kipling.
description: Mac is a puzzle. We have often wondered why he has never reached the age of discretion. Perhaps he is one of those fortunate persons, like Dr. Swartz, who retain their infant inclinations to an advanced age. He is very industrious—he always has too much to do to get any work done. He doesn't believe in wasting his time, and, therefore, comes to classes late to avoid getting there ahead of time. Mac conducts a convenience bureau for the C. E.'s. His desk contains everything known to the draftsman, and the C. E.'s appreciate his thoughtfulness in bringing all those things for them to use. However, he is a true and loyal friend, and may become an engineer some day if he can settle down to work—and leave colored chalk and fancy designs alone.

name: Macfarlane, Charles Edward
motto: "I would give all my fame for a pot of ale and safety."—Henry V.
description: Bonnie Prince Charlie of Chemicalico, the youngster who, when he grows up, is going to be a chemical engineer—or brakeman, as the present destruction of test tubes indicates, is chiefly renowned for his great genius in discovering new and delectable compounds. He daringly mixes C₁₀ H₂₂ O₁₁ with COF₂E₂ and T2 and boldly drinks of the result. A musician he is, being the composer of the Copper Suicide Blues, which have taken these parts by storm. So we have him, not afraid of odoriferous chemical combinations—therefore, devoid of fear. Always in good spirits and ever ready to lend a hand, he is a happy combination of blue eyes and Scotch grin. As one elderly lady put it, "Mr. Macfarlane is such a sweet boy."

name: Mallalieu, William Cassell
motto: "What's in a name."—Romeo and Juliet.
description: You've heard of the man from Kalamazoo Who ate too much hot celery stew But let us change your point of view And introduce Bill Mallalieu. When you've said your "How d'ye do." And "Mallalieu, now how are you?" You'll find he lives on caribou And if it were the thing to do He'd wash it down with Schlitz's brew. Behold he looks like Sidney Drew And dances like Bill McAdoo, In love he's like bold Rhoderick Dhu We know he loves to bill and coo And writes impassioned billets doux When he's done this a time or two We fear he'll meet his Waterloo His girl may tell him to skidoo Hard luck, old chap, and so adieu.

name: McCleary, Oscar Wood
motto: "I am a music-maker And I am a dreamer of dreams."—Arthur O'Shaughnessy
clubs: R. O. T. C., F. So.
Sergeant R. O. T. C., So.
Y. M. C. A. Cabinet, F. So. J.
description: Oscar Wood McCleary is a pure esthete. He is a lover of all forms of art, of music, painting, poetry, singing, checkers, and of beautiful women. But sure we cannot blame him for being such a diligent seeker after the esthetic, for after all, it is he, and not "the madding crowd," who most fully enjoys life. But with all of his poetic feats of imagination, his matchless soarings into the purple realms of the empyreal, he is at times a very practical fellow. Just ask him what he thinks of certain students or of a certain lovable professor, and you'll find that Oscar will, unlike many of the suave hypocrites of the modern age, tell you his frank opinion, unabashed by any threatening consequences. Mother Green said, not so long ago, that perhaps a new school of poets is going to arise; a school which will fuse together all the splendor of our age into a product unparalleled by anything that has gone before. Mac may be one of the shining lights in this new Renaissance.

name: William Lee Merriken
motto:
"He would not, with a peremptory tone,
Assert the nose upon this face his own."—Cowper.
clubs: R. O. T. C., So. S.
Y. M. C. A. Cabinet, So.
J. S. Secretary, Y. M. C. A. So.
Treasurer, Y. M. C. A., S.
description: Wild Bill—the perfect example of an enigma, a riddle which is as hard to solve as the differential calculus verse of Kreymborg. We have worn out "Webster" in the attempt to find a word that most adequately describes this elusive bit of clay from Roland Park. It is his great ambition to wield the gavel—presumably in a police court. Indeed, the length of time it takes him to find himself and his words, is nothing short of—well it would be deleted anyway. However, Bill is "a man for a' that," who has college spirit and plenty of it. He supports every activity that he possibly can. For four years he has worked with the track squad and his moral support is to be commended. If everyone were to follow his example, we would have no fault to find. Be confident and self-assertive, Bill, and success is bound to be yours.

name: Harold Holt Mersereau
motto: "Above the vulgar flight of common souls."—Murphy.
clubs: Mandolin and Glee Clubs, F. So. S.
Y. M. C. A. Cabinet, So. J. S.
Captain R. O. T. C., S.
description: At last this very rare and almost extinct Mesozoic specimen of an ichthyornis has been shot by the camera and mounted along with the other more or less invaluable fauna of this chewing-gum, cider-cursed age of sobriety. It must pain one of Harold's loftiness to see himself amongst such a heterogeneous crowd. He who is of the elite of Brooklyn—horrors no! of New York!—ever strides through the hurly-burly of this world with a supercilious air. It is rumored that he is a social service worker. What a paradox! But soft! Perhaps he is—among the planets. We are at a loss to know how to account for such strangeness, unless it be the strenuousness of preparation which the pre-med. course demands. Despite all this, to those of us who know Mers., his friendship is something to be sought for and his sphere is in the same plane as ours. It will be with pleasurable pride that we watch his meteoric rise to fame.

name: Ferdinand Meyer
motto: "Full of strange oaths and bearded like a bard."—As You Like It.
clubs: Varsity Lacrosse, F. So. J. S.
Manager Class Lacrosse Team, F. So.
description: Cover up defense! I got the man! Then some clever stick work and Captain Meyer takes the ball away from his opponent, runs half-way up the field and shoots to Sam Silberstein. Ferd then starts to move slowly toward goal with his stick dragging above the ground as if it weighed a ton. After arriving at his destination he chats with Morley and the rest of the bunch until there is some more action. Besides his lacrosse ability Ferd has other accomplishments, for instance, he has accomplished what he has tried to do these many seasons, namely, a noticeable mustache. He can also make the hill up to Gilman Hall in that green roadster of his on high, fool the profs into believing he is a good student and a hard worker and run up to New York in the bargain every other week.

name: Morriss, Henry Hayles
motto: "Too fair to worship, too divine to love."
description: There's no denying it Harry is a devil. He could get uproariously tight on one Bronx cocktail, and talk about the "spree" for months afterwards. Then, too, he can swear like a trooper,—or rather a trooper's wife, and does it quite naturally. Instead of guardian angels, fashionable patronesses watch over Harry,—at least he watches over them,—and we know to our certain knowledge that he has "Klots" on the brain. Success in every adventure to Harry's mind represents a long list of these beneficent and formidable beings. Harry hobnobs with all the society editors in town, and thought seriously of playing Betsey Patterson for the News-Letter. All fun aside, Morris is an enthusiastic and loyal son of Hopkins. He has done excellent work as circulation manager of the News-Letter and in the Dramatic Club, of which he was the founder.

name: Henry Edward Niles
motto: "At school I knew him—a sharp-witted youth, Grave, thoughtful, and reserved among his mates, Turning his hours of sport and food to labor, Starving his body to inform his mind."—Old Play.
clubs: Associate Editor, The News-Letter, So. S.
Member of R. O. T. C., F. So.
Executive Committee Social Science Club, So. S.
Y. M. C. A. Cabinet, S.
description: Now we come to old man serious himself. The grave, elongated face that stares vacantly at you here typifies the social scientist, the Y. M. C. A. fiend, and the News-Letter exchange all in one. Niles has contrived to make his influence a potent one in everything he tackles, and but for the handicap of a perhaps over-retiring nature might have risen still higher in the regard of the class. One lasting monument to his endeavors is the Social Science Club, which owes its existence to the untiring efforts of a small group of thinkers of which he was probably the most active. His editorial work has been of high caliber, illustrating the thoroughness which is his most striking characteristic. All he needs is a little leaven of cheeriness to brighten him, and the Baltimore Bar will possess yet another ornament of the name of Niles.

name: William Knight Norwood
motto: "Having quitted the Borders to seek new renown, Is coming by long quarto stages to town." —Moore: Comment on Sir Walter Scott.
clubs: Track Team, F., So., J., S.
description: Let it be repeated in a whisper: Bill comes from Windsor Hills, where the vagrant tripper dashes to and fro (and often sideways). He is noted for his pink cheeks and his ability to utter the most startling expressions in his ordinary conversation without changing gear. This blue-eyed youth spent last summer in Labrador with the Eskimos, where he learned the secret of keeping warm on cold days. We are therefore no longer moved to pity when we see his dryad form cavorting around the cross-country course. (Bill is really some runner). It has been whispered that our hero has gained considerable fame in the scientific world by his egg-standing experiments. It was he that found that eggs will stand nearly ten days in a jar atop the bookcase, provided that the room is not unnecessarily warm, and that the human occupants do not weaken too quickly. We don't know why, but we love everything about Bill, even the way he stands on one foot.

name: Orne, Stephen Williams Jr.
motto: "Little dew-drops of celestial melody."—Carlyle.
clubs: Secretary, Y. M. C. A., So.
description: When the instructor just begins to settle down to his lecture topic, Steve can be seen speeding into the room on his tip-toes, at the same time trying to adjust his tie or turn down the collar of his coat. Steve is one who usually has an opinion to express on any subject but he is always willing to let you speak first. As a classmate he is of the ideal sort—not the least obtrusive, possessed of sound judgment, loyal to his friends, and never taking offense over petty matters. His disposition should have been put into a man of twice his size, or rather, Steve should have been gifted with a form commensurate with his disposition; we can assure you that he would have been of some size. When he is not performing some difficult analysis or doing research for Dr. Thornton he is usually following in the path of the Alchemists—namely, making hard cash. All in all he is a very busy man. We wish you all the luck in the world, Steve!

name: Paige, Edmund Robert
motto: "A bright renown shall be enjoyed By those whose virtues claim reward."—Byron.
description: We tried hard to get something on Ed., but had to give it up in despair. We are forced to the conclusion that he is a gentleman, through and through. Grief and perplexities melt before his soothing "That's all right, old feller!" and his lecture notes never fail in time of stress and strain. He doesn't pester us like Andrae and Weaver do, and he always can be counted upon for one of those regular laughs when you spring one from your "private stock." Likewise, when there are any lab reports to be worked up, Ed. is right on the job. As a side line, our hero tickles away at a mandolin, which he keeps in tune and at home most religiously. Every other week or so he brings another song-sheet along from downtown, humming over the chorus with such evident delight that we never tear off a protest. Although "Peggy" is too modest to join the Glee Club, he is always on hand whenever it or the Cotillon Club has a dance.

name: Phillipy, Lester Newton
motto:
"Be calm in arguing: for fierceness makes
Error a fault, and truth discourtesy."—Herbert.
description: Due to the reactions accompanying the formation of the S. A. T. C., there was precipitated upon the class in the Junior year a somewhat pessimistically optimistic individual who claimed the distinction of being the only C. E. Junior at Hopkins. This precipitate, dissolved in the class, was called Phil and he immediately began to exert tremendous energy in talking to Charlie and his familiar. Phil came from Gettysburg College and was one of the Gregory martyrs at Hyattsville. At this last place he distinguished himself by effectually proving his financial ability—he saved more than one month's salary. We can hardly hold his Gettysburg descent against him; and from the way he has tried to tell John H. and Pagon where to get off, we predict a successful though argumentative career for him.

name: Rank, James Lindley
motto: "I hear thy shout, I know what thou wouldst say."—Hovey.
description: Lindley, better known as Pop, only joined us this year; consequently, we can say little of his private or social career before. He tells us that his desire when first coming to Hopkins from Gettysburg was to major English, but, as he says, "It ain't no use for a fellow to take up a subject when he has to first develop a liking for the Professor." He still writes quite a little, but not for the English Department. Very regularly white envelopes leave his domicile and pink ones return. After all, Pop is a very conscientious worker—his marks tell that tale. But even more remarkable than this, he is contemplating taking graduate work next year. All of us would like a Ph. D., but few of us have the energy to earn one.

name: Saiontz, Leon Robert
motto: "His speech was a fine sample on the whole Of rhetoric which the learned call rigamarole."—Byron.
clubs: Chairman Executive Committee Chess Club, S.
description: Saiontz is one of the three-year men in our class. He entered the University in the fall of 1917, spent a year at Columbia, and came back here for his third and final year. It has so far been rather hard for mortal eye to discern what subject has had the honor of being Saiontz' major; however, it is sure to be Science (Saiontz). (The author of this article may as well state that this is the first decent pun he has heard since he read Twelfth Night under that far-famed Prof. of the Black Bag). Saiontz's cardinal principle is: Obey rules only so far as they do not cause you any inconvenience. Thus the class in Labor Problems time and again missed his illuminating presence merely because it met at that ungodly hour—9:30 A.M. As a matter of fact, things came to such a point that the instructor threatened him with sundry dire misfortunes if he did not make his visits more frequent. As to his future, nearly everyone feels that a certain pet propensity of his would make him a good lawyer (?). Success to him!

name: Scott, Carl Gustave
motto: "Omnia vincit labor."
clubs: Editor, Bureau of Athletic Publicity, S.
description: This is Carl Gustave Scott, who hails from the state of gigantic, carnivorous mosquitoes. Part biologist and part psychologist, he is ever ready to tackle the most baffling problems pertaining to the brain or to the mind. Having delved in many volumes on heredity, evolution and sociology, his own mind is a rich storehouse bulging with interesting and surprising facts. Name an animal, and he will give you its position in the animal kingdom, its ancestors, and its descendants. Ask him what consciousness is, and he will philosophize for hours at a time. Carl takes particular pride in his technique, which is exemplified in all his laboratory work, and like all good technicians he does not hesitate to upbraid the indifferent student for what he terms "sloppiness." From a family of doctors, he too is going into medicine and will undoubtedly develop into a great scientist and physician.

name: Schaffer, Alexander Jonas
motto: "When all is said and done, he's a devil and an Ostrich, and an orphan child in one."
description: This masculine edition of a Daisy Ashford bereft us of his smiling presence last year, a deprivation from which we have not yet recovered. His career has been sensational in the extreme and has caused the sob sisters of Baltimore's yellow sheet to rave overtime. At the age of three he was reading ancient Greek manuscripts and Phoenician tracts in the original, at seven he was graduated from City College, and at eleven or thereabouts he left Hopkins at the urgent solicitation of Drs. Finney and Howell and, were it not for the Maryland laws, we are sure he would now at the tender age of thirteen be practicing medicine. And yet he has always found time to grin, to play basketball, and to engage sub rosa in sundry other less laudable occupations. Keep grinning, Buck, and you'll get there sure.

name: Shaw, Harry Breckinridge
motto: "Sergeant, take his name!"—Alexander the Great.
clubs: Captain R. O. T. C., S.
description: This young man has aspirations of becoming either a sanitary engineer or a teacher of Physics. The big question before him is: Which is the worse? Of course, we all understand that he will be a success no matter which he chooses, for look how he climbed the ladder of military promotion in the R. O. T. C. He has risen to the high rank of captain of C company, but he would surely have become a general or something if he had not been busted when a corporal (in his freshman year) for cussing Captain Thompson. There stands one achievement, however, that makes us look at him with awe. For the sake of a devoted young lady in Frederick, he has kept far from the wiles of the Baltimore Maidens.

name: Edward Wilson Sickel
motto: "Conspicuous by his absence."—Tacitus.
description: Sic is one of the members of our class who is going to make the name of '20 prominent in the medical world. He completed his undergraduate course in three years with a good standing and at the medical school is still keeping up his good work. Sic does not, however, devote all his time to studies. For two years he was one of the mainstays on the varsity baseball team as well as playing an important part on the class athletic teams. Probably his most daring act while at the University was the part he played in blowing up Gilman Hall and part of Homewood when he was a member of the R. O. T. C. Sic has always taken an active part in the different University activities and the best wishes of the class go with him as he starts out upon his life work.

name: Samuel Silberstein
motto: "He thought as a sage, though he felt as a man."—James Beattie.
clubs: Varsity "H", Lacrosse, F., So., J., S.
Class Banquet Committee, So., J., S.
description: Sam is well known around the University because of his stellar work on the lacrosse field and as a leader in undergraduate affairs. Winning a position on the lacrosse team in his freshman year, Sam has held down the same berth ever since, contributing largely by his brilliant playing to the success of the Black and Blue. As president of the Student Council he has handled the different questions which arose, with considerable skill and good judgment. It was due to this ability that he was elected a member of the Athletic Board in his senior year. To look at Sam you would take him to be a quiet and orderly individual, but one has only to ask a member of the lacrosse team who has been to New York with him, to learn that at times he is like a ship on a rough sea. No one, however, deserves the honor of O. D. K. more than Sam, and our best wishes go with him as he starts out on his life work.

name: Smith, Louis Golden
