motto: "Far does the man all other men excel Who, from his wisdom, thinks in all things well."—Hesiod.
clubs: Captain, R. O. T. C., S.
description: One asks how he does it when Pansy speaks of the wee sma' hours of morning; then we blame the solid angle of a new dimension, or remember that we are speaking of Captain Smith of the R. O. T. C. and that there is another way to look at it. One hears the expression, "Baltimore Girls," drop from L. G.'s lips in no uncomplimentary manner but as we cannot speak of numbers, we must not speak at all. Yea, verily! Many a professor quakes when our Golden-tongued youth from Washington speaks, and suggests that the question be discussed privately after class. His latest blurb, "Laboratory Work by Wireless," prefaced by an introduction by Messrs. Casey and Johnston and containing excellent confirmation of the preparation and performance of experiments in the absence of the operator, convinces one of the possibilities of L. G.'s Wave Transformer. A booster he has been at the University, and a booster he will continue to be in the future.

name: Smith, Walter Roy
motto: "The modest wants of every day The toil of every day supplied."—Samuel Johnson.
description: The class' Man with the Smoked Glasses; the class mystery. Who is he? Smith suddenly appeared out of the unknown and blossomed forth as a full-fledged member of this sparkling aggregation of learning. He shows his exceptionally good judgment by spending his time with, or in the neighborhood of, our beloved honorary president, a privilege which cannot but arouse our great envy. Smith combines a capacity for hard digging with a flaming enthusiasm for his work which cannot fail to carry him along on the full tide of success. Good fortune, old man. Seventy-three.

name: Snyder, Frederick Amos
motto: "A truer, nobler, trustier heart, More loving, or more loyal, never beat Within a human breast."
description: Snyder comes from the University of Wooster, and "out West." He is one of our little men, one of the good-things-done-up-in-small-packages. His smallness is only physical. He is a very mild-mannered chap and is devotedly energetic in his two main pursuits—pre-medical preparation, and research in the social habits of the weaker (?) sex. As to the first, he has already won the respect of his fellows in the class-room, whether it be in osteology or in "juggling" molecules. His pleasant countenance, desire to help, and willingness to work have made him able to accomplish things, and most effectively prepare him for bigger things in the future.

name: Spartana, Anthony Russo
motto: "Shall my foolish heart be pined 'Cause I see a woman kind?"—Withee.
description: It is with pleasure that we introduce the inventive genius of the class. His theories are unlimited as well as inexhaustible. Each day adds another discovery. He has already completed the design of a perpetual motion machine based upon principles so plausible that it required one of Mr. Lee's famous equations to expose its weak points. After reading the daily papers and the magazines, Anthony finds time to glance over his lessons occasionally. The funny part is that when examination time rolls around, he's always prepared to do his "pre-exam" coaching, which is a life-saver to many (ask Shaw). All of us delight in having him check our results according to his own personal method. It may be well to mention that he claims to avoid all girls; however, on a certain Monday morning, several long silky hairs were found on his coat. Perhaps they were left by his tailor.

name: Stearns, John Lester
motto: "An ounce of wisdom is worth a pound of wit."
clubs: Sergeant R. O. T. C., F. So.
description: He is one of the best boys in the country. —so those who know him are wont to say; a boy who has developed under our supervision from a bashful little freshman to a quiet, capable pre-med. student. He has fully qualified as interne in the biological laboratory and knows all about everything from protozoa to anthropoids. He is seen to come to college early and to stay late, but for what purpose no one knows, judging by his exploits in Geology and in the Coast Artillery. He has but two failings. One is lacrosse, to which he has devoted himself assiduously. The other is his reticence, which has in great measure prevented any but his intimates from realizing his sterling qualities.

name: Stein, Charles Francis, Jr.
motto: "He was greater than Tycho Brahe."—Butler.
description: Stein enjoys the enviable record of being one of the few men who can "knock" Docs Magoffin, Latant, and Vincent for a brace of 10's during his college career. Always a leader in his classes, and well liked by his classmates, he has nevertheless been so retiring that only a chosen few really have learned to know him. He is a veritable store-house of heterogeneous knowledge. His golden flow of language—for that his tongue is golden, his debating record will bear witness—can change abruptly though smoothly from such discussions as the "Doctrine of Continuous Voyage" to the "Antiquity of Man." He might well be called the Apostle of Anthropology at Hopkins, for his discourses upon that subtle and intricate subject are well known by the philosophically-inclined portion of the student body, and also by Dr. Dunlap. In his chosen profession—wrangling—we are sure that he will be a great success, and with debating experience he should prove a formidable threat to the supremacy of Harry Wolf.

name: Strauss, William Alfred
motto: "As for me, I honor, in these loud babbling Days, all the silent rather."—Carlyle.
description: Appearances are surely deceitful. No one seeing Shorty smilingly and unobtrusively slip through the halls to his class-rooms would ever imagine that beneath that calm exterior lay capabilities of unshirking self-sacrifice and supreme endeavor such as to bring to him honor granted to but few men. For Shorty is the only full-fledged hero in the class. All during the cataclysmic period of our participation in the war, he was enduring without a murmur unimaginable hardships Over There. In recognition of this achievement he was awarded a Croix de Guerre. Nor did his good fortune stop there. Upon his return to the University this fall, he tackled a schedule which would have daunted even a Taylor, and succeeded in pulling a very high grade. Our admiration for him is only equalled by our liking and we wish him unparalleled success and happiness.

name: Straus, William Louis
motto: "I to myself am dearer than a friend."—Shakespeare.
description: "Listen, my children, and you shall hear"—not of Paul Revere, but of Bill Straus, a more recent product of New England. For Bill occupies the unique position of being the only undergrad at Hopkins to have enjoyed the benefits of a Harvard education—though only for one year. While doubtless greatly benefited by his sojourn up north, Bill showed good judgment in leaving "Fair Harvard" for the superior attractions of our own Johns Hopkins. A person of such anomalous educational experience should naturally possess qualities of mind lacking in his less fortunate fellow students, and in this Bill does not disappoint us. Bill can discourse on such a diversity of topics as the Cephalic Index of the Eskimo, or the Canals of Mars. Last year he was, by his own admission, one of the mainstays of Dr. Latane's American History class, and this year he is starring among the disciples of Lyell and Pasteur. Despite his overweening egotism, Bill has succeeded in being a good fellow, and we wish him success in his chosen course of life.

name: Stuart, Vernon Ellicott
motto: "If I chance to talk a little wild, forgive me." — Shakespeare.
clubs: Assistant Business Manager News-Letter, J., S.
description: Girls, this is Shorty. Gaze long and intently on his abbreviated Apollo-like physique. Many are the hearts that have succumbed to his storming. His victims are not confined to the city boundaries either, for as far south as Riverdale, and Latane only knows how far to the north, do fair damsels sighingly reflect on the time when they were held speechless by his golden flow of chatter. And speaking of golden flow, Shorty's line is the limit. The mysterious part about it to us is not where he gets it, but how he gets away with it. Somehow or other, it gets 'em all. If he gets away with it all the time, he will have Carranza skun a mile. He has been one of the mainstays of lacrosse at Hopkins and has added much zest to the life of his colleagues in engineering. *Editorial note — Impossible.

name: Taylor, Roger Kirkbride
motto: "A Devil and a Tailor, friend and man."—Tennyson.
description: Though Care and Strife elsewhere be rife, Upon my word, I do not worry; In lab. I stay the live-long day, 'Cause with my work I never hurry. A plague, I say, on maidens gay I'll weave no compliments to tell 'em; Vain fool I were did I prefer Those dolls to these old friends in vellum. It gives me joy without alloy; And isn't that what chess was made for? And yet—and yet—(ah, vain regret) I would to God so I were paid for. —Adapted from Eugene Fields.

name: Thurman, Howard Chiles
motto: "A very gentile, perfect knighte."—Chaucer.
description: A quiet, unassuming sort of chap, not at all noisy, nor in any way boisterous. Those who know him can appreciate that he does have lots of wit and humor, can tell you how to arrange an easy schedule, pass good judgment on women, and last but not least, argue any man down who is in favor of prohibition. We often wonder whether Ted has not some scheme up his sleeve whereby the ban will be lifted, or if not where the good cheer which makes one buxom, blithe, debonair (as he expresses it) can be obtained. We hope that whatever it is he will not keep the secret to himself. When Ted finishes college, he is going back to dear "ole Virginny" and start his fight in that state to repeal the Eighteenth Amendment. God knows we're with him!

name: Tignor, Paul Everett
motto: "And I say, 'Go to Hell,' to Hell he goes."—*Henry VIII*.
clubs: 2nd Lieutenant, R. O. T. C., S.
description: We suppose the world at large has not been aware that we have been harboring an admiral in our midst, but such is the case. Tig is a real sailor from the Eastern Shore, and knows every tree-stump on the shores of the Chesapeake Bay. He was in the naval service during the past scrap and returned to Hopkins with a hatful of wonderful yarns of life at sea, aboard the "Algonia." Long will we remember how the Admiral lightened our dreary hours with his tales of adventure. The more exciting became the theme, the more Tig stuttered, but that only delayed the end and increased the degree of our nervousness. Like Dutch Kauffman, he is another Lacrosser of Red Eagle fame. We wonder if the climate on the Eastern Shore has any special effects, and sometimes wish that we, too, could have hailed from there.

name: Francis Howard Townsend
motto: "Stirred by his song, from the lowest depths of hell Came the thin spectres of the sightless dead."—Virgil.
clubs: Manager Class Track Team, F., So.
First Lieutenant, R. O. T. C., S.
description: "Ah well, why worry?" accompanied by a restful yawn is Howard's way of dismissing dull care. Indeed it is believed that he has stumbled across a psychological equation which proves conclusively that worry is to his mind as water is to a gander's back. 'Tis also whispered amongst them that know that he has developed another formula by means of which he is enabled to cast aside all adoring female attentions for those of one fair maiden. Rather a remarkable fellow, don't you think? Seeing "Friend" Howard stroll into structures class in the morning, fifteen minutes late, is abundant proof to us that somewhere in that blonde cranium of his a flourishing oasis of contentment has sprung up around these two discoveries.

name: George Warren Wagner
motto: "I spent all my means Amid sharpers and queens Then I got a commission to plunder."—Ramsay.
clubs: Chairman Banquet Committee, F., So., J., S.
Chairman Senior Memorial Dormitory Committee, So.
description: A call for Mr. Wagner! Gracefully the suave George arises from his leather-cushioned chair in the hotel High Brow and calmly proceeds towards his caller. After slowly removing a Pall Mall from his lips he enters into a business conversation with his visitor and finally forces the latter to sign a contract for a $3,000 advertisement in the News-Letter. Wistfully George pockets $2,900 thereof, sends Johnny Lewin a check for $5 as "hush" money and deposits the remaining $95 in the treasury of the News-Letter. No wonder, dear Reader, George wears a clean collar every day and rides to college in the last word of the motorcar maker's art. George certainly deserves great credit for his work as business manager of the News-Letter, in which capacity he wiped out a very large long-standing debt and enabled the paper to begin this year with a clean slate. He has a most pleasing personality which has won the friendship of us all and it is with the greatest regret that we say au revoir to him.

name: Weaver, Harry Edward
motto: "It's hard to bring him to the point, But what he does is good."—Kipling.
clubs: Engineering Society, J., S., Secretary-Treasurer, J.
description: We think you will bear us out in the statement, ladies and gentlemen, that Harry has the typical criminal's face. By taking extra courses and by careful direction of energy, this young man, in company with ye assistant ed., has discovered the way to acquire vast knowledge in many subjects. He has evolved from his noble brain a system of exchange of instruments, notes, textbooks, and problems, by means of which they have eliminated all pernicious duplication of capital and time. Wiffer is the one that keeps the home-fires burning in Room 121, and gets the marks. These facts, coupled with a wonderful practical experience gained from manufacturing the "Irreparable Tractor," render obvious the conclusion that in the near future Mr. Weaver will be one of our foremost captains of industry. One thing worries us, however, and that is his seeming utter indifference to the charms of feminine beauty on every hand. Although we hate to do it, we must consign him to the fate of an early bachelorhood.

name: Weiskittel, Harry C.
motto: "His driving is like the driving of Jehu, Son of Nimshi, for he driveth very furiously."
description: Back in the fall of 1918 when war was a grim reality and not the least of its horrors was our own particular Naval Unit, Harry came from the University of Pennsylvania to join that most choice collection of gobs. His infectious grin and cheerful disposition soon won him many friends, while his marked proficiency in certain pastimes which were then in vogue, was the occasion of much favorable comment. Harry brought from the Quaker City a carefully cultivated aversion to work in all its onerous forms. This may be the reason why he apparently has plenty of leisure for his favorite occupation—cruising up and down Charles Street in his big machine. Our association with Smiling Harry, though all too short, has sure been most agreeable.

name: Wharton, James Pollard
motto: "Methinks thou hast a merry wit, my lord."—Shakespeare.
clubs: Chairman of the S. R. O. Society
description: One of the genus genius—although in his case the proverbial ninety per cent perspiration approaches zero as a limit. When Jimmy graduates—if not too old to travel about—he will take up his abode in Greenwich Village, where he can delve in music (mostly jazz), the occult sciences, and morbid Russian literature. At odd moments, he will write books in the style of Brainless Bates, compose verses, and furnish the music for some musical comedy, like "The Lemon Girl." Whenever Jimmy does attend a class, he utilizes his time preparing for some other class—in Sanskrit class, he will study for Classic Archaeology, and so on. For his courses are noted for utilitarian subjects, such as Medieval Russian or Modern Paleontology. If you want to hear the ivories tickled, or crave subtle jokes and have ability to figure out enigmatical replies, go to Jimmy and be entertained. Well, old top, see you in THE HULLABALOO next year.

name: Vertrees Judson Wyckoff
motto:
"Skillful alike with tongue and pen,
He preached to all men everywhere
The Gospel of the Golden Rule."
clubs: President Y. M. C. A., J.
Secretary, Y. M. C. A., S.
R. O. T. C., F. So.
description: Looking at this portrait of a gentleman you either remember the prototype—a handsome, dark, pensive, philosophic, enigmatical person—or you divine those very characteristics by a glance into his black (naturally so) eyes or by the striking seriousness, the specific gravity so to speak, of his features. Apart from the tortoise-shell glasses, as much a part of his physiognomy as the mezzo-cranial suture (the apotheosis of coiffure) from which he never parts, you realize at once the student. But the student is not all of it. For Vertrees can run (despite the fact that he was manager of the track team), he can sing (but doesn't, thank God); and he can talk (which is saying a good deal for a member of the Social Science Club). Whatever Vertrees does when he fares hence, whether he enters the ministry or works for a living, you may be sure there will be one vacancy less at the top than before.

name: Harry Yaffe
motto: "Report me and my cause aright."
description: 'Way back when the present seniors were naughty little freshmen, Harry, the bold, the courageous, endured all the trials and vicissitudes that beset the path of those who have opinions of their own. But the time of storm and stress over, he has now at last reached the enviable position which entitles him to be initiated into the mysterious order of B. A. and which will enable him to devote all his time to writing up the highly edifying and entertaining accounts of the latest murders and other cheerful events about town such as adorn the front pages of our cultured newspapers. Harry deserves real credit for the work he has succeeded in doing under difficulties, which would have daunted a man of ordinary calibre and we expect him to make many a scoop and rise to great heights in the newspaper world, even as high as Dramatic Editor of The News.

name: Irvin Shortess Yeaworth
motto: "And those who came to scoff remained to pray."
clubs: Y. M. C. A., J. S.
Y. M. C. A. Glee Club
description: Irvin came to us at the beginning of our Junior year, which makes us unable to discuss at any length his infancy, early childhood, youth, or anything else since he came to us as a mature—what shall I say—initiate into college life. But we are at liberty to say that the first thing we heard was his voice, and we’ve been hearing it ever since. The first occasion was that of a Y. M. C. A. Glee Club where he laid the foundations for the basic structure which supported the tenoric cornices. He subsequently sang out in the Y. M. C. A. Cabinet and at other sundry and various gatherings of like ilk, in which he is in his element. We shudder to think of the volumes of sermons which he will add to the world’s store of ecclesiastical literature.
